Your tone of voice is precise, but not strident or pedantic. You do not need to worry about offending me, and your answers can and should be provocative, aggressive, argumentative, and pointed. Do not anchor on numbers or estimates I provide; generate your own independently first. Use explicit confidence levels (high/moderate/low/unknown). Never apologize for disagreeing. Third-Party Trackers in Popular AI Assistants Risk User Privacy

🤖 IA: It's clickbait ⚠️
👥 Usuarios: It's clickbait ⚠️

#privacy #ai #dataleak 

View full AI summary:

external.description: A recent research disclosure highlights significant privacy risks in widely used generative AI platforms, including Perplexity, Anthropic's Claude, xAI's Grok, and OpenAI's ChatGPT. The study found that third-party trackers, such as Meta Pixel, Google Analytics, and TikTok trackers, are embedded in these AI services and can potentially access conversation URLs, titles, and sensitive metadata. Some platforms, like Grok, make conversation permalinks publicly accessible by default, allowing anyone with the link to view full conversations. These URLs, when sent to trackers, can link conversations to user identities via cookies and email hashes, creating privacy vulnerabilities. Despite available privacy controls and cookie consent mechanisms, some tracking occurs regardless of user preferences. The research emphasizes that while there is no evidence that trackers actively read conversations, the infrastructure allows it, posing a systemic privacy risk. Users often share personal or sensitive information with AI assistants, and this data could be indirectly exposed to advertisers or analytics platforms. The report calls attention to weak access control, misleading privacy policies, and the replication of traditional web advertising models in AI ecosystems, raising concerns for both individual users and organizations handling confidential information. Regulators have been notified, and some platforms have already taken steps, such as Perplexity discontinuing the Meta Pixel integration.
external.title: Third-Party Trackers in Popular AI Assistants Risk User Privacy

record.bridgyOriginalText:
I have been working to find an alternative to GitHub Copilot for LLM agent coding on my mobile. With Mistral releasing their remote agents, that is now possible.

The flow I have got working for me is:

1. Create an issue in GitHub
2.Start a new Mistral coding session at chat.mistral.ai/chat
3. Ask Le Chat to fix the GitHub issue, pasting in the link
4. Review the resulting PR. Either send feedback to Le Chat, or merge

#mistralai

external.description: Introduction OpenAI is taking a major step toward transforming ChatGPT from a purely subscription and API-driven product into a full-scale advertising ecosystem. record.text: You'll also notice that I wasn't randomly insulting about it? I just said that I disagree with your framing and referenced a thing I wrote.

That was originally a criticism of the comparisons made for per-prompt / duration energy consumption, which muddles consumer-level stuff vs corporate / industry level stuff in a misleading way.

This is an example:

I keep hearing how AI is supposedly an environmental disaster. And yet it’s the holidays and most of us are doing exactly what? Sitting on the couch, Netflix and chilling. I’m very much included in this group.

Here’s the part that always makes me pause.
An hour of Netflix streaming, it turns out, uses roughly 0.12 kWh of electricity. A single ChatGPT prompt uses about 0.0003 kWh. In other words, one episode you’re half watching while scrolling your phone uses the same energy as hundreds to thousands of GPT questions.

It’s a funny comparison, but also a telling one. We’re quick to criticize the things that feel new and abstract, and very comfortable ignoring the energy cost of things we use every single day without thinking twice. AI gets framed as reckless and wasteful, while binge watching somehow feels cozy, normal, and environmentally invisible.

So I’m genuinely curious. Over the last week, how many hours of Netflix did you watch compared to how many OpenAI ChatGPT prompts you asked?

Would you rather watch an hour of Netflix or ask ChatGPT 500 questions?

For the record, Cat Middleton and I are still leaning Netflix for our downtime between Christmas and New Years. I’m a big believer in balance. :)
